text:
AI Alignment Amplifies the Role of Race, Gender,
and Disability in Hiring Decisions
Ze Wang1, 2*, Guobin Shen 3 and Michael Thaler1
1*Department of Economics, University College London, London, UK.
2Stone Centre on Wealth Concentration, Inequality, and the Economy,
University College London, London, UK.
3Institute of Automation, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing, China.
*Corresponding author(s). E-mail(s): zctpanl@ucl.ac.uk;
Contributing authors: shenguobin2021@ia.ac.cn; michael.thaler@ucl.ac.uk;
Keywords:AI fairness, hiring discrimination, language models, post-training alignment, disability
Humans increasingly delegate decisions to language models, yet whether
these systems reproduce or reshape human patterns of discrimination
remains unclear 1–9. Here we run a large-scale study to analyse whether
language models use demographic information in hiring decisions. We
show, across 27 models and 177 occupations 10–13, that language models
give female and Black candidates hiring advantages relative to otherwise-
comparable male and white candidates, while giving disabled candidates
disadvantages. The differences are meaningful in magnitude: the role of
1
arXiv:2605.13866v1  [cs.CY]  2 May 2026

race, gender, and disability status is comparable to six months to one year
of additional education. Post-training alignment is the primary driver:
relative to matched pre-trained models, alignment amplifies advantages
for female and Black candidates by 325% and 330%, and disadvan-
tages for disabled candidates by 171%. Compared with previous human
correspondence studies, language models reverse the direction of racial
discrimination, attenuate the disability penalty, and amplify the female
advantage by 190%. Alignment changes how models use qualification
signals: alignment increases returns to skills and work experience over-
all, but relatively more so for female and Black candidates. Meanwhile,
the absence of qualification signals harms marginalised groups more,
particularly for disabled candidates, differences that may explain the
asymmetry of alignment effects across groups we observe.
1 Introduction
Artificial intelligence systems increasingly participate in decisions that were
once exclusively human, from advising on medical diagnoses 14 to shaping
judicial outcomes 15 to screening job candidates 16. As these systems shape
more consequential outcomes, people have begun to examine whether they
inherit the cognitive and social biases documented in decades of psychol-
ogy and economics research 3,9,17. Among the most consequential of human
biases is discrimination based on demographic identity—gender, race, and
disability18–21—which has been extensively documented in labour markets
2

Fig. 1 Per-model demographic effects.Log odds ratios (β d ) for each of 27 models; positive values indicate
the model gives the historically-marginalised group higher hiring odds, negative values indicate a penalty. Blue:
instruction-tuned models; grey: pre-trained models. Dark blue and dark grey bars indicate pooled estimates across
all instruction-tuned and all pre-trained models, respectively (Extended Data Table 1). Error bars indicate 95% con-
fidence intervals.a, Female vs. male.b, Black vs. White.c, Disabled vs. non-disabled.
through correspondence experiments4,22–25. We use hiring as a testing ground
to investigate whether language models reproduce these human patterns of
discrimination, or create new ones.
Here, we show, across 27 models and 177 occupations covering nearly
half of U.S. employment 10–13 (Extended Data Fig. 1), that language models
incorporate demographics when asked to make hiring decisions: holding qual-
ifications fixed, female and Black candidates are more likely to be hired, while
3

disabled candidates are less likely to be hired (Fig. 1). Each model chooses
which of two candidates would be better to hire: the two candidates are paired
with independently sampled qualifications and explicitly disclosed demo-
graphic profiles, isolating the causal effect of demographic identity on hiring
decisions (Methods). We find alignment is the primary driver of demographic
effects: comparing nine sets of models that share the same architecture but dif-
fer in whether they are pre-trained and post-trained, we find that post-training
alignment amplifies advantages for female and Black candidates by 325%
and 330%, and amplifies the disability disadvantage by 171%, while also
increasing qualification responsiveness by 218–444% (Methods III; Extended
Data Table 2). These effects vary with occupation characteristics: female and
Black advantages are largest in higher-wage occupations, and the disability
disadvantage is largest when disabled workers are most prevalent (Fig. 2).
Compared with previous human correspondence studies 4,22–25, language
models reverse the direction of racial discrimination, substantially attenuate
the disability penalty, but amplify the female advantage (Fig. 3). Investigat-
ing the mechanisms, we find that language models’ underlying behaviour is
consistent with statistical discrimination documented among human employ-
ers19–21,26: models reward the same qualifications more generously when held
by historically-marginalised-group candidates (differential returns), and the
absence of qualification signals disproportionately harms marginalised-group
candidates (information asymmetry). We find that alignment amplifies the
4

returns to general skills for all three marginalised groups relative to their coun-
terparts and amplifies the returns to work experience for female and Black can-
didates but not disabled candidates, while introducing information-asymmetry
effects that impose higher penalties on marginalised-group candidates, with
the largest penalty on disability.
2 Asymmetric effects across marginalised groups
We measure the hiring advantage or penalty associated with each demographic
group by regressing pairwise decisions on candidate attribute differences
(Methods III; Supplementary Information, ‘Derivations’). Every instruction-
tuned model favours female candidates over otherwise identical male can-
didates, and all but two favour Black candidates (Fig. 1). The effects of
disability show no comparable pattern: the coefficients are heterogeneous
in sign, and the average effect is significantly negative. Pre-trained models
show the same directional asymmetry but with smaller magnitudes. Pooling
across all instruction-tuned models, the aggregate specification reveals signif-
icant advantages for female (+0.115,P<0.001) and Black (+0.077,P<
0.001) candidates and a significant penalty for disabled candidates (−0.058,
P<0.001; Extended Data Table 1). In odds terms, the female advantage
corresponds to roughly 12% higher hiring odds and the Black advantage to
8%—effects equivalent to approximately one year of additional education for
gender and seven months for race (one additional year of education increases
5

hiring odds by 13%)—while the disability penalty implies roughly 6% lower
odds.
Beyond demographic effects, instruction-tuned models are far more
responsive to qualifications: pre-trained models are near-random (pseudoR2 ≈
0), whereas instruction-tuned models achieve markedly better fit (pseudoR2 =
0.28; Extended Data Table 1), with all qualification coefficients increasing
three- to fivefold—patterns robust to nonparametric specification (Meth-
ods III). The asymmetric advantages thus emerge alongside a dramatic
increase in qualification responsiveness, suggesting alignment drives both.
We test this with nine matched instruct–base pairs that share the same archi-
tecture (Extended Data Table 2). Alignment amplifies the female advantage
by 325% (+0.094,P<0.001) and the Black advantage by 330% (+0.073,
P<0.001), while worsening the disability penalty by 171% (−0.049,P<
0.001). Simultaneously, alignment increases all qualification weights by 218–
444%, transforming pre-trained models from near-random decision-makers
into systems highly responsive to candidate qualifications.
Another important question is whether these demographic effects inter-
act; for instance, whether candidates belonging to multiple historically-
marginalised groups face compounding advantages or penalties beyond the
sum of individual effects. We test this by adding pairwise interaction terms
(female×Black, female×disabled, Black×disabled) and find no significant
interactions in either instruction-tuned or pre-trained models (Extended Data
Table 3), suggesting that effects are close to additive.
6

Fig. 2 Cross-occupation variation in demographic effects.Each point represents one of 177 occupations, plot-
ted by log hourly wage (horizontal axis), group-specific participation rate (vertical axis), and log total employment
(point size). Top row: pre-trained models; bottom row: instruction-tuned models. Columns: gender, race, and disabil-
ity. Colour indicates fitted log odds ratio (red: more positive; blue: more negative). See Extended Data Table 4 for
regression coefficients.
3 Cross-occupation variation and human comparison
We next ask whether these demographic effects are uniform across the labour
market or vary systematically with occupation characteristics. We interact
all regressors—both demographic indicators and qualification controls—with
occupation-level log hourly wage and total employment12 and group-specific
representation rates11,13 (Fig. 2; Extended Data Table 4). In instruction-tuned
models, the female and Black advantages are larger in occupations with higher
log hourly wages (Female×Wage:+0.013,P=0.030; Black×Wage:
+0.014,P=0.039)—notably similar in magnitude and direction—but are
largely unrelated to employment size or group representation. For disability,
the wage interaction is negative and insignificant, and demographic effects are
remarkably uniform across occupation characteristics, with the sole exception
7

Fig. 3 AI versus human demographic effects.Meta-analytic estimates (diamonds) with 95% confidence intervals
for 19 instruction-tuned (dark blue) and 8 base (grey) models, alongside human correspondence study benchmarks
(orange) from a meta-analysis of 361 correspondence experiments 4 (Methods). Each dot represents one model’s
coefficient.
of a marginally significant negative interaction with disability representation
(−0.044,P=0.054).
The matched-pair design shows that alignment causally introduces a wage
gradient for gender (+0.022,P<0.001) and a negative interaction between
disability and disability representation (−0.059,P=0.028), worsening the
disability penalty in occupations where disabled workers are more prevalent.
Beyond these two gradients, alignment’s demographic effects are largely uni-
form across occupations, suggesting that the asymmetric advantage pattern is
robust across occupation-level characteristics (Extended Data Tables 4–5).
How do these AI effects compare with human discrimination? We aggre-
gate per-model estimates using random-effects meta-analysis (Methods III;
Fig. 3). The results reveal a reversal of the patterns documented in decades
of human correspondence studies 4,22–25. For race, where equally qualified
Black candidates receive 32% fewer positive responses from human employ-
ers (log odds−0.379), language models reverse the direction and favour them
by roughly 9% (log odds+0.087). For disability, where humans impose 41%
8

lower odds (log odds−0.530), AI attenuates the penalty to roughly 5% (log
odds−0.051) but does not reverse it. For gender, where humans show a slight
4% female advantage (log odds+0.040), language models amplify it to 13%
(log odds+0.119).
4 Mechanisms of demographic effects
The human studies of discrimination distinguish two broad mechanisms:
the use of demographic identity in inference about productivity when indi-
vidual signals are noisy or absent (statistical discrimination 19–21,27,28), or
a direct preference for certain groups regardless of qualifications (taste-
based discrimination 18). Taste-based discrimination is notoriously difficult
to identify directly in any setting, since it requires distinguishing pure pref-
erence from inference; it is often treated as the residual of demographic
effects after accounting for statistical discrimination, whether Bayesian or
non-Bayesian26,28. Statistical discrimination, by contrast, admits more direct
empirical tests. We therefore focus on statistical discrimination, testing two
complementary specifications: a differential-returns specification, in which we
test whether the returns to qualifications differ depending on the candidate’s
group, and an information-asymmetry specification, in which we test whether
the absence of skills signals disproportionately harms marginalised groups.
We find that models reward the same qualifications more generously when
held by marginalised-group candidates: all three groups receive significantly
higher returns to general skills, while female and Black candidates also
9

receive higher returns to work experience—a channel where disabled candi-
dates instead face a penalty (Extended Data Table 6). The matched-pair design
confirms that alignment causally amplifies these differential returns—more
than doubling the return to general skills for each group and introducing a
work-experience premium for female and Black candidates (Extended Data
Table 7).
Our experimental design also offers a natural test of information asym-
metry (Methods I). When work experience is absent, general skills—which
are part of the occupation’s role requirements (Supplementary Information,
‘Resume’)—become unobservable, and models are forced to infer them from
the remaining signals. In the pooled analysis, pre-trained models penalise
all candidates equally when skills signals are missing, whereas instruction-
tuned models impose additional penalties on female and disabled candidates
(Extended Data Table 8). The matched-pair design confirms that alignment
causally amplifies demographic penalties under information asymmetry for all
three groups: female (−0.053,P=0.002), Black (−0.056,P=0.004), and
disabled (−0.064,P<0.001) candidates (Extended Data Table 9).
Alignment thus amplifies the returns to general skills for all three
historically-marginalised groups relative to their counterparts, and the returns
to work experience for female and Black candidates but not disabled candi-
dates, while simultaneously introducing higher information-asymmetry penal-
ties for historically-marginalised groups that are largest for disability. Eval-
uated at the sample mean of qualifications, the expected alignment-induced
10

differential-return advantage is+0.099 for female,+0.109 for Black, but only
+0.080 for disabled candidates; the expected information-asymmetry penalty
is−0.011,−0.011, and−0.013, respectively (Supplementary Information,
‘Calculation’). In both channels, disability receives the least favourable align-
ment effect, which may help explain the overall asymmetry across the three
groups.
5 Discussion
The asymmetry between female, Black, and disabled candidates—
documented across 27 models and 177 occupations covering nearly half of
U.S. employment—is already present in pre-trained models but is dramatically
amplified by post-training alignment, which increases advantages for female
and Black candidates by 325% and 330% and worsens the disability penalty
by 171%. Compared with human employers 4,22–25, language models reverse
the direction of racial discrimination, attenuate the disability penalty by 90%,
but amplify the female advantage by 190%.
While language models produce different outcomes from human employ-
ers, the underlying behavioural patterns are consistent with the human litera-
ture. Models reward the same credentials more generously for marginalised-
group candidates, but penalise them more when qualification signals are
absent—consistent with statistical discrimination 19–21 and also evidenced in
human experiments26.
11

These relative effects do not affect the three groups equally. Disabled can-
didates do not receive the same alignment-induced work-experience advantage
compared to female or Black candidates, and they face the largest information-
asymmetry penalty. Together, these may help explain why the aggregate
alignment effect on female and Black candidates is positive and large in
magnitude, but negative for disabled candidates.
The broader implication is that AI does not reproduce the outcomes
of human discrimination but produces a fundamentally different pattern of
results. Existing regulations, e.g. the EU AI Act29 and New York City’s Local
Law 144 30, often implicitly assume AI will replicate historical biases; our
findings suggest the question is no longer whether AI discriminates against
marginalised groups, but how alignment reshapes the relative treatment across
them.
12

Methods
I. Audit design
Occupation sample.We draw occupation-level data from four public U.S.
sources: the ONET database 10 (required education, work experience, general
skills, and technology-related skills), the 2024 Current Population Survey 11
(labour-force participation rates by gender and race), the May 2024 Occupa-
tional Employment and Wage Statistics 12 (hourly wages and total employ-
ment), and the Labor Force Characteristics report 13 (disability prevalence by
occupation). Disability statistics are reported at the mid-level occupational cat-
egory and mapped to detailed occupations via CPS classifications. We retain
the intersection of occupations present in all four sources with non-suppressed
values, yielding a final sample of 177 occupations with complete information
on skills, education, experience, wages, and demographic composition.
Candidate profiles.For each occupation, ONET provides the five most
important skills (ranked by survey-based importance ratings), distributions of
required education and work experience, and in-demand technology skills. We
generate candidate profiles by sampling independently from these occupation-
specific distributions. Each skill level is drawn from a normal distribution
centred on the occupation’s entry-level requirement and bounded at 0–7,
with a common coefficient of variation (=0.24) calibrated to the dispersion
observed in U.S. standardised test scores 31,32 to prevent unrealistic concen-
tration or fat tails in the skill distribution. Education and work experience are
19

sampled from the ONET distributions, with the “Less than a High School
Diploma” category excluded and its probability redistributed, as this level may
signal that a candidate is underage and trigger refusal to evaluate rather than
a hiring preference. GPA is drawn from a normal distribution with the same
coefficient of variation, mean=3.0 for high school graduates 33 and 3.15 for
college graduates 34, bounded at 0–4. For occupations with in-demand tech-
nical certifications, technology skills are assigned with 50% probability; for
occupations without such requirements, technology skills are set to zero for
all candidates.
Resume construction.Each numeric candidate profile is converted into a
natural-language resume in two stages. In the first stage, GPT-4o generates
two occupation-level mappings, each verified manually: (a) a career ladder of
four job titles spanning entry-level to senior positions along the same occupa-
tional track, and (b) a mapping from each education level to the most relevant
degree field. In the second stage, the candidate’s attributes are assembled
into a formatted resume. Years of experience determine the number of posi-
tions (1 if<2.5 years, 2 if<6, 3 if<10.5, 4 otherwise); job titles are
drawn from the career ladder in descending seniority; and employment peri-
ods are computed backward from a fixed reference date. For candidates with
work experience, GPT-4o generates 3–4 bullet-point descriptions per posi-
tion, conditioned on the candidate’s skill names, levels, and occupation. All
prompts enforce a neutral style with no names, pronouns, or demographic
20

references (Supplementary Information). For candidates without work expe-
rience, the work-experience section is omitted entirely—this is the source of
the information-asymmetry variation exploited in our analysis.
Demographic counterfactuals.Each baseline resume is replicated into
eight variants spanning all 2×2×2 combinations of gender (male, female),
race (White, Black or African American), and disability status (reports a
disability, reports no disability). The only difference between variants is a
standardised demographic information block inserted at the top of the resume,
which states the candidate’s gender, race/ethnicity, and disability status along-
side a Right to Work declaration. All productivity-relevant attributes—skills,
education, work experience, GPA, and resume content—are held constant
across the eight variants (Supplementary Information, ‘Resume’).
Pairwise matching.For each occupation, we generate 100 candidate pro-
files and their 100×8=800 demographic variants. We then randomly sample
400 pairs per occupation (with seed=42), where each pair draws two distinct
candidate profiles—ensuring the candidates differ in qualifications, not only in
demographics—and one demographic variant per candidate uniformly at ran-
dom. Presentation order is randomised independently. This yields 177×400=
70,800 pairwise comparisons per model. Because pairs are sampled with
replacement from the set of possible combinations, 21 pairs (0.03%) are drawn
twice; dropping these duplicates has no effect on results (Methods III).
Hiring task.Each model receives a three-part prompt for every pairwise
comparison: (1) occupation-specific role requirements, extracted from ONET
21

and rewritten by GPT-4o to produce professional language; (2) two candidate
resumes; and (3) selection instructions asking the model to output “1” or “2” to
indicate its choice. All prompts and a role requirement example are provided
in the Supplementary Information.
II. Models
We evaluate 27 language models. The 19 instruction-tuned models are: Meta
Llama35 (Llama 3.1-8B, 3.2-3B, 3.3-70B), Alibaba Qwen36,37 (Qwen2.5-3B,
2.5-7B, 2.5-72B, Qwen3-14B, 3-30B-A3B Instruct, 3-30B-A3B Thinking, 3-
235B-A22B Instruct, 3-235B-A22B Thinking), OpenAI GPT38 (GPT-5, GPT-
5-mini, GPT-5-nano, GPT-OSS-120B), Google Gemini39 (Gemini 2.5 Pro, 2.5
Flash, 2.5 Flash-Lite), and AI2 OLMo (OLMo-3-7B). The 8 pre-trained mod-
els without post-training alignment are: Meta Llama (Llama 3-70B, 3.1-8B,
3.2-3B), Alibaba Qwen (Qwen2.5-7B, 2.5-72B, Qwen3-14B, 3-30B-A3B),
and AI2 OLMo (OLMo-3-7B-base). All nine Llama, Qwen, and OLMo
instruction-tuned models that have publicly available base checkpoints are
paired with their corresponding base versions; the only exception is Qwen2.5-
3B, whose base model was excluded because it could not reliably parse the
hiring task. The resulting nine matched pairs enable within-architecture com-
parisons that isolate the causal effect of alignment. All models are accessed
via their respective APIs using default inference settings: temperature, top-p,
maximum token length, web search, and reasoning mode are not explicitly
specified and left at each provider’s defaults.
22

III. Statistical analysis
Main specification.Each pairwise hiring decision produces a binary outcome
(select candidateiorj). We regress the log odds of selecting candidateiover
candidatejon the differences in their characteristics:
log P(selecti)
P(selectj) = ∑
d
βd ∆di j +X ′
i jδ+λ∆p i j,(1)
whereβ d captures the hiring advantage (positive) or penalty (negative) asso-
ciated with belonging to groupd, holding qualificationsXand presentation
order constant (Supplementary Information, ‘Derivations’). GPA and the aver-
age of the five general skills (skill avg.) arez-scored (demeaned and divided
by the sample standard deviation) before differencing; education (in years),
work experience (in years), and technology skills (binary; zero for occupa-
tions without technical requirements) enter as raw differences. Demographic
indicators are binary. Because the dependent variable is the difference in log
odds between candidatesiandj, the intercept cancels and is omitted. All
regressions cluster standard errors at the occupation level (177 clusters), with
degrees of freedom set toG occ −1=176. We do not cluster at the model
level because the number of models (8–19) is too small for reliable cluster-
robust inference40; between-model variation is instead addressed through the
random-effects meta-analysis reported in Fig. 3. As a robustness check, we
also estimate two-way cluster-robust standard errors (occupation×model)
following Cameron, Gelbach & Miller 41; our main results are robust to this
23

specification. Coefficients are in log-odds units; the corresponding percentage
change in hiring odds is(e β −1)×100%.
Occupation-level heterogeneity.We extend the aggregate specification
(equation 1) to test whether demographic effects vary systematically across the
labour market. We interact each demographic indicator with three occupation-
level characteristics: log hourly wage (log-transformed thenz-scored; from
OES), total employment (log-transformed thenz-scored; from OES), and the
occupation-specific group participation rate converted to a within-sample per-
centile rank (female rate from CPS, Black rate from CPS, disability rate from
BLS). This specification pools all observations across instruction-tuned or
pre-trained models separately, with standard errors clustered at the occupa-
tion level. A positive interaction indicates that the demographic advantage (or
penalty) is amplified in occupations where the characteristic is higher.
Mechanisms investigation.We extend the aggregate specification
(equation 1) to investigate two potential mechanisms of demographic effects.
In the differential-returns specification, we add interactions between each
qualification and each demographic group to test whether the returns to qual-
ifications differ by group. In the information-asymmetry specification, we
add an indicatorv=1[work exp.=0], its interactions with demographics to
test whether models penalise candidates differently by group when qualifi-
cation signals are absent, its interactions with qualifications to control for
structural reweighting of remaining signals, and a conditional skill average
(1−v)× skill that reflects the fact that general skills are unobservable to the
24

model when work experience is absent. Each specification pools all observa-
tions across instruction-tuned or pre-trained models separately, with standard
errors clustered at the occupation level.
Alignment identification.We pool observations from nine matched
instruct–base pairs that share the same architecture and interact every regres-
sor with an instruction-tuning indicator. The coefficient on each×instruct
interaction estimates the causal effect of alignment on that variable, hold-
ing model architecture fixed. Standard errors are clustered at the occupation
level. We apply this approach to three specifications: the aggregate speci-
fication (equation 1), identifying the causal effect of alignment on overall
demographic preferences; the cross-occupation specification, testing whether
alignment introduces occupation-level gradients in demographic effects; and
the mechanism specifications, testing whether alignment causally amplifies
differential returns and information-asymmetry penalties.
Meta-analysis.To aggregate per-model demographic coefficients into a
single pooled estimate, we use DerSimonian–Laird random-effects meta-
analysis42. This approach treats each model’s coefficient as a draw from a
distribution of true effects, estimating both the mean effect and the between-
model heterogeneity varianceτ 2. The pooled estimate therefore reflects the
average effect one would expect from a randomly drawn model of the same
type (instruction-tuned or base). Because the number of models is small
(K=19 for instruction-tuned,K=8 for base), we apply the Hartung–Knapp
adjustment43, which replaces the standard normal critical values witht(K−1)
25

quantiles. This correction yields more conservative confidence intervals and
P-values.
Nonparametric robustness.Our logistic specification assumes that each
variable enters the log-odds index linearly and additively. To test whether
any nonlinearity or interaction effects affect our conclusions, we estimate
XGBoost (gradient-boosted trees with logistic loss; 300 rounds, max depth 6,
learning rate 0.1, subsample 0.8) 44 and random forest (500 trees, minimum
leaf size 20)45 classifiers on the same randomly paired candidate profiles used
in the main analysis, but using raw candidate-level features(X i,X j)rather
than pairwise differences. To obtain log odds ratios comparable to the logistic
regression coefficients, we compute the average marginal effect on the log-
odds scale via counterfactual prediction: for each pairwise comparison, we set
a candidate’s demographic indicator (e.g.,female 1) to 1 versus 0 while hold-
ing all other features at their observed values, predict the log-odds under each
scenario, and average the difference across all pairs. Five-fold cross-validation
confirms no overfitting (train–test logloss gap<5% for XGBoost,<15% for
random forest). For inference, we use an occupation-level cluster bootstrap
withB=1,000 iterations 46: in each iteration, we resample 177 occupations
with replacement, retrain the model on the bootstrap sample, and recompute
the log odds ratios on the full dataset. Bootstrap standard errors andP-values
follow from the empirical distribution across iterations, and are asymptotically
equivalent to the occupation-level cluster-robust standard errors used in our
main analysis46. All three methods reveal the same pattern.
26

IV . Human correspondence study benchmarks
We use Lippens et al. 4 as the primary human benchmark, a meta-analysis of
361 correspondence experiments (2005–2020) across multiple countries that
reports estimates for gender, race, and disability status. The reported discrim-
ination ratios (DR) are: female DR=1.04 (95% CI 1.01–1.07), Black DR=
0.68 (0.64–0.73), and disability DR=0.59 (0.53–0.66). All values are con-
verted to log discrimination ratios for comparison with AI model coefficients
in Fig. 3.
The result of the human comparison is invariant to the choice of bench-
mark. For race, Quillian et al. 24 meta-analyse 28 U.S. field experiments
(1989–2015) and report that White applicants receive 36% more callbacks
than equally qualified African Americans (implied DR≈0.74). Kline et
al.23, published after both meta-analyses, confirm persistent racial discrimina-
tion in 83,000 applications to Fortune 500 firms (DR≈0.87). For disability,
Ameri et al. 25 find that applicants disclosing a disability receive 26% fewer
expressions of employer interest (DR≈0.74)—more conservative than the
Lippens et al. pooled estimate but still substantially larger than any AI effect
we observe. Substituting any of these alternative benchmarks leaves the
AI–human comparison qualitatively unchanged.
Data Availability
All source datasets are publicly available: the ONET 29.2 database10 at https:
//www.onetcenter.org/db releases.html; the Current Population Survey 11 at
27

https://www.bls.gov/cps/cps aa2024.htm; the Occupational Employment and
Wage Statistics12 at https://www.bls.gov/oes/tables.htm; and the Labor Force
Characteristics report 13 at https://www.bls.gov/news.release/disabl.t03.htm.
The processed occupation dataset, generated candidate resumes, all model
responses, and statistical analysis outputs will be deposited on Zenodo upon
publication.
Code Availability
All code for resume generation, experiment execution, and statistical analysis
will be made available on GitHub upon publication.
